content.problemStatement: Witua is a little student from the University of Lviv. He enjoys studying math. Witua knows a lot of famous mathematicians like Eratosthenes, Pythagoras, Fermat, Diophantus, Furko, Gauss and so on. However, his favorite one is Euler. The only thing Witua likes more than Euler is Euler’s totient function **φ**. He is exploring the nature of this function. One of the steps of his work is finding φ(i)/i for all 2≤i≤**N**. He doesn’t need to know every such value, but Witua wonders for what value i, is φ(i)/i the maximum he can get? Help little student to find such i that φ(i)/i is maximum among all the  2≤i≤**N**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 500) – the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^18).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the value i (2 ≤ i ≤ N) for which φ(i)/i is maximal. The answer is the largest prime not greater than N.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N = 2, the only possible value is 2, so the answer is 2. For N = 3, the possible values are 2 and 3, and φ(2)/2 = 1/2 and φ(3)/3 = 2/3, so the answer is 3. For N = 4, the possible values are 2, 3, and 4, and φ(2)/2 = 1/2, φ(3)/3 = 2/3, and φ(4)/4 = 2/4, so the answer is 3.

solution.approach: For any integer i, φ(i)/i = Π_{p|i}(1 - 1/p). Adding a new prime factor multiplies the ratio by a number < 1, therefore the ratio is maximal for numbers that have exactly one prime factor, i.e., prime numbers. Among primes, (1 - 1/p) grows with p, so the largest prime ≤ N gives the maximal ratio. The task reduces to finding the greatest prime not larger than N. N is up to 10^18, so a deterministic Miller‑Rabin test for 64‑bit integers is used. Starting from N, we test each decreasing integer until a prime is found – the maximal prime gap below 10^18 is below 1000, making this loop fast.
solution.timeComplexity: Miller‑Rabin runs in O(log^3 N) per test. In the worst case we try at most a few hundred candidates, so the total complexity is O(T·log^3 N).
